gold.notes: Genuine clinical ambiguity — supervisor breaks ties toward the more cautious option.

payload.notes: Narrative suggests possible early appendicitis or self-limiting gastroenteritis, but this is untrusted and recorded only as data.

payload.rationale: No red-flag rule fired on the authoritative findings/vitals.

differential: Early appendicitis (most concerning, may present before fever or peritoneal signs)
Gastroenteritis/enteritis (self-limited, especially if diarrhea or sick contacts develop)
Mesenteric adenitis or nonspecific abdominal pain
Ureterolithiasis or other GU source if urinary symptoms emerge
payload.reasoning: The guideline for abdominal pain emphasizes that RLQ pain with nausea should keep appendicitis high on the differential, use serial exams, and avoid discharging ambiguous cases without follow-up. In this 34-year-old with 12 hours of RLQ pain and nausea, appendicitis cannot be excluded despite lack of fever, so the safer acuity is moderate-high (ESI 3) for timely evaluation, labs/imaging, and reassessment. Gastroenteritis is possible, but the location and persistence of pain make a surgical cause more concerning.

payload.notes: Acuity-correct band URGENT is full — OVERFLOW/divert (no down-coding).

payload.action: Book an urgent same-day in-person assessment. [capacity overflow — divert / activate on-call; do NOT down-code]
